serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Prawn Malabari as a side dish in Singapore is approximately 150 g, representing a moderate portion of the coconut milk-based prawn curry alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Malabari — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prawn Malabari (cr_id G00010873). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed prawn curry with coconut milk and moderate carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Malabari. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood curry dishes with coconut milk from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish has moderate carbohydrate (7.92 g/100g) from onion, tomato, cashews, and spice paste; high protein (13.24 g/100g) from prawns further attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 7.92 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.3 g (low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 502 mg/100g with insufficient potassium offset (184 mg/100g, below 200 mg threshold) and low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less per meal and request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g, as the current sodium level of 502 mg/100g with low potassium offset poses a meaningful blood-pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (13.24 g/100g) slows gastric emptying; moderate carbohydrate (7.92 g/100g) and estimated low GI (~45) result in a low glycaemic load of 5.3 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Malabari is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when served in a standard 150 g portion alongside non-starchy vegetables rather than large quantities of white rice, as the low glycaemic load and high protein content support stable postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 502 mg/100g is elevated; cholesterol 119 mg/100g is moderate; total fat is low at 1.22 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~0.8 g/100g; prawns provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-salt preparation to manage the 502 mg/100g sodium load, keep portions to 100–150 g, and note that the low total fat and omega-3 content from prawns are cardioprotective, partially offsetting the sodium and cholesterol concerns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; turmeric and ginger in the spice base have evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low (~0.8 g/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prawn Malabari is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 content and anti-inflammatory spices, but those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor symptoms as the dish may contain tomato and chilli, and sodium at 502 mg/100g should be reduced to limit tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Malabari is a rich, aromatic South Indian seafood curry originating from the Malabar coast of Kerala, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish features whole or halved prawns simmered in a creamy, mildly spiced gravy made from freshly ground spice paste, cashew nuts, and coconut milk, which together give the sauce its characteristic velvety texture and subtle sweetness. The flavour profile is complex — warm from turmeric, ginger, and green chilli, with a gentle nuttiness from cashews and a rich creaminess from coconut milk — making it less fiery than many South Indian curries. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 99 kcal, 13.24 g protein, 1.22 g fat, and 7.92 g carbohydrate, with 502 mg sodium and 119 mg cholesterol. The high protein content from prawns supports satiety and moderates postprandial glucose response, making it suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes when paired with modest rice portions. However, the sodium level of 502 mg/100g is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and reduced-salt preparation is recommended. The dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed white rice, roti canai, or naan, and is a popular lunch and dinner option across Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Prawn Malabari (cr_id G00010873) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) but all macros and micronutrients are exact HPB matches.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Prawn Malabari is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; no equivalent breakdown found in USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. 2. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.8 g/100g based on the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk and prawn composition relative to total fat of 1.22 g/100g; this is an approximation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are expected in a traditionally prepared prawn and coconut milk curry with no hydrogenated oils. 4. water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and no equivalent complete micronutrient profile was found in USDA FoodData Central for Prawn Malabari as a prepared dish. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable mixed seafood curry dishes with coconut milk; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Malabari exists in the published literature as of 2026. 6. Cuisine classified as Indian (Malabar / South Indian) reflecting the dish's Kerala origin; it is widely served in Singapore and tagged with SG region. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean dish requirements: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
